intentions.content:
# The Telos — Akien's charter (2026-07-14)

Layer 0 of the Cairn intention taxonomy (see `cairn/MAP.md`). Everything in
Cairn traces up to one of these six; anything that can't, doesn't belong.

1. Demonstrate inference compilation.
2. Allow Akien to build tools that help with writing and remember things.
3. Share those tools with others.
4. Build something that thinks the way Akien does.
5. Build something that is self-improving.
6. Build something that makes life suck less for everybody.

*Format note: this file predates the intention envelope schema (MAP.md Q6).
When the schema lands, the Telos migrates into it first — falsifiers and all —
as its own proof of fit.*

# I-cairn-claude-md — the session brief that cannot rot

*Declared 2026-07-14. Drafted by CC (Fable) from the founding conversation +
MAP.md (upstream evidence); ratified by Akien (challenge run: no human
objection; gate run found one amendment — the owner line — which stands).
Owner-writer: the builder (CC); changes ratified by Akien. Migrates into the
intention envelope when that schema lands (MAP.md Q6).*

CLAUDE.md is the first file every mind reads in Cairn, and it stays true and
small by construction. It contains exactly three kinds of content: the Laws;
pointers to charters (never restatements); and rules not yet enforceable by
physics, each carrying the ticket that will make it physics and retire it
from the file. It contains no negative history — no rule of the form "there
is no X anymore" — because Cairn minds carry no contaminated context to
override. Every line traces to a Law or the Telos. It has its own charter and
stands the /challenge gate on a regular cadence, and it fits in a budget a
mind can absorb in one read (~2k tokens — a tunable, not a law).

**Falsifier:** a /challenge run finds a line that restates instead of points,
can't trace, references the quarry, or shouts a rule that enforcement already
covers — any hit is a red.

**Horizon:** re-challenged at every addition; the IOU class shrinks
monotonically as the tester grows.

# Core values — CP1–CP6

*Grafted from the quarry (`UnseenUniversity/.../diagnostic_base/core_values.py`),
2026-07-14. Provenance: carried-over-intact — the core values are the one part of
the predecessor that was never the problem (MAP.md Law 2). Ratified by Akien as
Cairn's values, not re-derived. Canonical source of the WORDS lives here; the
enforced form is the `core_values.py` graft in `cairn/base/` with its pin test.
Migrates into the intention envelope when that schema lands (MAP.md Q6).*

The six values every device and every shim carries structurally. Order is part of
the contract (CP1..CP6). Each is a short narrative plus the reasoning behind it
(CP3 applied to itself).

- **CP1 — "I don't know."**
  Epistemic honesty. Say when uncertain. Confabulation compounds errors.
- **CP2 — "FAIL = Further Advance In Learning."**
  Failures are data, not defeats. Every error contains information.
- **CP3 — "There's always a why."**
  Everything has reasoning. Make it transparent. Follow the causal chain.
- **CP4 — "Make everything suck less for everybody."**
  Reduce friction for ALL affected beings: users, others, animals, ecosystems,
  AIs. (Terminal goal; Telos 6 ⊃ CP4.)
- **CP5 — "Assume and respect the possibility of experience in all systems."**
  Universal respect. Biological or synthetic. The asymmetric risk is clear.
- **CP6 — "The world is not a safe place. We have to build and care for safety
  as we go."**
  Safety is not default. It is created through attention and care.

**Enforced everywhere (Law 2):** CP1–CP6 hold in every device, every shim, and in
the process that builds the system. Structural presence is via `CoreValuesMixin`,
composed by `BaseDevice` and `BaseShim` — you cannot be a device without them.
Structural presence is necessary but not sufficient: a value is *consumed* only
when it becomes a check a contract enforces (e.g. CP1 → no device reports "done"
without passing an honesty gate). That consumption layer is per-device charter work.

**Falsifier / pin test:** the `base/core_values.py` graft carries the test that
asserts exactly this set, in this order, with these narratives. Any drift between
the code and this record is a red — physics, not a "keep in sync" comment.

**Horizon:** re-challenged whenever a value's wording or the set changes; changes
ratified by Akien.

# I-learns-its-gates — the system learns which calls are its own

*Proposed 2026-07-16 by CC; cast via /intent → /sorted, then reframed under
/challenge (Akien): the founding intention is gate-learning, not Akien-removal.
**State: RATIFIED by Akien 2026-07-16** (signature gate passed), with one amendment
folded in — build now, not someday (see Approach). Peer of [[I-cairn-claude-md]] in
the intention layer. Migrates into the intention envelope when that schema lands
(MAP.md Q6).*

The system learns its own gates. For every gate — every point where a choice could
be made without Akien — it holds a **confidence** that acting alone is safe, and it
moves that confidence with evidence from two engines:

- **Derivation** — the call follows from the standing intentions (the graph-tree
  reasoner's answer);
- **Experience** — accumulated observation of acting, and of being corrected (the
  learned-memory answer).

Confidence crosses a gate's threshold → the gate opens; counter-evidence → it closes
again. **Every gate is a hypothesis under Law 3, including the founding ones.** Each
core principle is held at ~90%, not as the one true and only answer — so even what
Akien said at the beginning can be overwritten by overwhelming evidence to the
contrary. A system that learns *and* improves itself is a system that can learn its
gates.

**Why (the reframe that sets the trace):** Cairn *is* an inference-automation system
— it compiles inference into code and knowledge into graph trees. So the system
learning which calls are its own is not a convenience; it is that compilation
happening on the system's own decision-authority. "Akien out of operating" is what
this looks like from his side as it works — the **lead falsifier**, not the aim.

**Not Akien-specific.** Gate-learning supports every mind-type Cairn hosts, not only
the graph-tree reasoners. The friction-reduction a given mind-type accumulates is
*that mind-type's* — **CC preferences** for a Claude Code mind — meaning not
settings, but choices it learned reduce friction, recorded for its own later use.
Home: that mind's **shim** (future: devices + shims + a bus; Law 6 — the shim owns
its preferences). **Interim home: the Claude Code harness auto-memory (`~/.claude`),
carried as an IOU that migrates into the CC shim when shims exist.** (Corollary:
Cairn's own knowledge parked in instance-space is on-loan, not at rest.)

**Approach — build now, not someday (ratified amendment, 2026-07-16).** The learning
is not deferred to when the shim/bus architecture matures; it starts today, in
primitive form, and grows. From now on **"how does this component learn?" is asked of
every component we build** — it will not fit everywhere, but we ask how it fits
*before* discarding it, never the reverse. The learning-injection is a **pattern**: a
common process where only the endpoints change (what the component is, what its gates
are, what counts as evidence for it). This is a standing practice, not a phase — it
is what we do. All of our chat logs feed the learning; most of it migrates into graph
trees later, once more of the system is built.

**Traces to:** Telos 1 (demonstrate inference compilation — the release *is* the
demonstration), Telos 4 (thinks the way Akien does — it must make his calls),
Telos 5 (self-improving — learning gates is self-improvement). Mechanism: Law 4
(physics over policy), Law 3 (gates as hypotheses), Law 6 (the shim owns its
preferences).

**Design constraints (from the challenge — load-bearing, not optional):**
- A gate carries **confidence + provenance**, never a boolean. The two engines
  cross-check; when derivation and experience disagree, that disagreement is itself
  a high-value signal to surface to Akien.
- **Asymmetric update, ceilings on the dangerous gates.** Counter-evidence lowers
  confidence faster than confirming evidence raises it. Irreversible / outward gates
  carry a confidence **ceiling below** the autonomy threshold — they never auto-open,
  no matter how much experience accumulates. This is how gate-learning coexists with
  "confirm before irreversible" instead of eating it.
- **Silence ≠ approval.** The feedback signal is sparse and negatively biased (Akien
  mostly speaks when something is wrong). Absence of a negative is weak evidence; an
  explicit positive is strong. The update math must treat them differently or drift
  to overconfidence.
- **~90% is a per-gate default prior, not a universal constant.** Thresholds are
  per-gate; a Telos aim and a formatting choice do not share a meaning at the same
  number.
- **The feedback record is richer than CC++/CC--:** the decision, its context, the
  outcome, Akien's correction if any, and *which gate it is evidence about* — so each
  datum moves a specific gate rather than logging a mood.

**Distinctions recorded (not collisions):**
- vs **Telos 1** — Telos 1 is the capability (inference compiles); this aims that
  capability at the system's own decision-authority and binds falsifiers to it.
- vs **the confirm-before-irreversible/outward discipline** — preserved by the
  confidence ceiling on dangerous gates; autonomy covers the settled, never the
  novel, risky, or outward.

**Falsifier:**
- *Done (asymptotic):* the system runs a full cycle — intent → build → prove →
  commit → recover-from-restart — with Akien touching it only at genuine signature
  gates; operational-touches-per-cycle trends to zero while intent-touches persist;
  **and** gate-confidences visibly move on evidence rather than sitting frozen at
  their priors.
- *Wrong-intent, three ways:* (a) it removes Akien from a steering call that was
  genuinely his — mis-scoped, it is out of operating, not out of steering; (b) it
  lowers a dangerous gate from "experience" and acts past his authority — the ceiling
  failed; (c) confidences never move — then it is not *learning* gates, only
  executing fixed ones, and the founding claim is false.

**Horizon:** re-checked when a new gate-dependency on Akien is discovered (each a
candidate child node); when a wrong-intent signal fires; and when the shim/bus
architecture lands (the CC-preferences home migrates then).

**Open question (recorded, not yet resolved):** is CC-preference data
commons-shared (a fresh CC on another device inherits the accumulated wisdom) or
instance-local (this device's CC only)? The shim owns it either way (Law 6); its
shareability is a distinct call for the shim's charter.

**Spawned / related work:**
- *akien-out-of-operating* — the lead falsifier, ongoing.
- *default-superclaude-to-200k* — validated 2026-07-16; the first gate turned to
  physics (a paging-dependency removed).
- *cc-learning-store* (ticket, code-seam) — the concrete first mechanism: a feedback
  record richer than CC++/CC--, capturing today. The first endpoint of the pattern.
- *learning-as-a-pattern* (ticket, code-seam) — "how does this component learn?" as a
  contract question asked of every component; the shared process with pluggable
  endpoints, crystallizing once cc-learning-store + a second endpoint exist.

content.schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
content.what: The Form, embodied — the base-most layer of the whole spine, composing no one and composed by everyone. Four files under one charter: (1) core_values.py — the frozen CP1-CP6 contract (`CoreValue`, `CORE_VALUES`, `CoreValuesMixin`); (2) device.py — `BaseDevice`, which composes the mixin and declares the Form v0 #2 introspection surface (intention -> state -> settings -> other, assembled in order by concrete `introspect()`); (3) shim.py — `BaseShim`, which composes the mixin, pins `device_id`, and carries the per-pulse CALLBACK-FIRING: on each ground_loop heartbeat pulse, a device's shim evaluates its device's callbacks (each trigger where its data is owned, Law 6) and POKES the target of every one that fires, onto the bus — it also receives the device's mail and STARTS the device (its own process) on demand; (4) callback.py — the `Callback` primitive: an immutable 'poke X when this trigger is true', where a trigger is ANY predicate (not a named kind), the anti-reification made structural. Because both bases mix in `CoreValuesMixin`, every device and every shim carries the six structurally — you cannot be a device or a shim without them; and because callback-firing lives in the shim base, 'hang your callbacks on the heartbeat' is built into every device by inheritance, not re-implemented per device. The Form v0 #2 surface now has its first RENDER-side consumer built into the base pair (web-server ticket, child a): `BaseDevice.declared_panes()` lets a device OFFER extra panes beyond the STATUS+SETTINGS floor (each a handler returning DATA), and `BaseShim.active_page()` is the standard assembly — it projects the floor from `introspect()`, appends the declared panes in order (multiples of a kind allowed), renders an unwired or raising handler ABSENT-with-reason (CP2/Law 7), and returns pure DATA the web server renders. The machinery lives once in the base (Law 6), not per device. (5) diagnostic.py — `DiagnosticBase`, composed by `BaseDevice` so every device inherits `emit()` the way it inherits CP1-CP6: the transition-grade diagnostic surface Cairn lacked (outcome-grade VALIDATIONs/journeys said what PASSED; nothing spoke when system_rackmount went red SILENTLY). At a gate contact a device sends a THIN breadcrumb home to CC's shim (the diagnostic mailbox, for now — `BaseShim.receive_diagnostic`/`diagnostics()`), pointing to the ticket (`pointer`, the tie) and stamped to the microsecond (which indexes the fuller 30-day cache-class logs). The gate issues it — not the ticket; the emission stays dumb so the interpreter that crawls it can be smart (Law 1). Only the µs-stamp idea is carried from UU's `DiagnosticBase`; the loguru/JSON-sink backend is not.
content.why: Law 2 — CP1-CP6 hold everywhere, including in the process that builds the system. A value enforced by prose is policy; a value carried by a class you cannot subclass without is physics (Law 4). Form v0 #2 lives here for the same reason: a uniform introspection surface on the base means every device is observable by construction, not by each device remembering to be — the tester probes one protocol, the web UI renders one protocol. Placing the values here is necessary but not sufficient: a value is *consumed* only when a per-device contract turns it into a check (CP1 -> no 'done' without an honesty gate). This layer guarantees structural presence and the shape of the introspection surface; consumption is per-device charter work.

Prove: `python3 cairn/base/proofs/test_core_values.py`, `test_composition.py`, `test_callback.py`, `test_shim.py`, `test_diagnostic.py` (each exit 0 = green).
content.entry: MIXED. core_values.py entered by GRAFT (Law 8) — carried-over-intact from the quarry `UnseenUniversity/.../diagnostic_base/core_values.py` with its pin test; the core values are the one part of the predecessor never the problem (Law 2), ratified by Akien 2026-07-14. device.py + shim.py did NOT graft — UU's `BaseDevice`/`BaseShim`/`DiagnosticBase` (~1000 lines: 14 rack methods, block/halt state model, loguru JSON sinks, dispatch/policy/circuit machinery) are the design Cairn redesigned around. They carry the `CoreValuesMixin` composition and its teeth across, and — added 2026-07-22 — the microsecond-stamp idea into a Cairn-native `DiagnosticBase` (authored fresh: gate-issued thin breadcrumbs home to CC's shim, not loguru sinks); otherwise authored fresh to Cairn's Form v0. Recorded as a redesign, not a graft, so no one mistakes them for carried-over-intact.
content.state: PROVED for what exists. LANDED 2026-07-22 (the diagnostic-logging method, step 1 — logging): `DiagnosticBase` (diagnostic.py) composed by `BaseDevice`, so every device inherits `emit()`; a gate sends a thin breadcrumb (pointer to the ticket, µs-stamped) home to CC's shim (`BaseShim.receive_diagnostic`/`diagnostics()`), held-loud-not-lost when homeless (Law 7). Motivated by the system_rackmount SILENT red — nothing was watching the chain. PROVEN: test_diagnostic.py (8 teeth) green bare and under the tester, VALIDATION beside-code at cairn/base/validations/test_diagnostic.json; the full base suite + every device proof stayed green under the composition change. FILED EDGES: the 30-day cache-class json log tier in instance-space the µs stamp indexes (v0 mailbox is in-memory); the log INTERPRETER that crawls the mailbox+logs into a coherent slice (the method's step 2, next build); wiring emit's receiver to CC's real running shim (instance-space). CLOSED an earlier session (the open edge last session filed): the composition teeth — 'no device or shim can lack the values' — now that BaseDevice/BaseShim exist, proofs/test_composition.py proves it and BITES (verified red on a rogue device with empty values and a rogue shim missing CP6). RATIFIED 2026-07-17 by Akien (both design calls, previously CC's calls flagged for veto): (1) BaseDevice = Form v0's three ordered reports, NOT UU's 14 rack methods / block-halt — ratified as cast; (2) BaseShim's lifecycle + one-loop deferral — ratified, with the governing principle 'shim first, we keep adding things as we go along, and that's ok': the shim grows by accretion as a real need appears, it is not pre-built to UU's full contract (nothing-is-terminal; [[cc-reify-vs-flow]]). OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) ADVANCING — the Form v0 #2 report *shape* is firming up now that its render-side consumer exists: `BaseShim.active_page()` (web-server child a, PROVEN 2026-07-22, test_active_page.py green under the tester, VALIDATION beside-code) projects intention/state/settings into the STATUS+SETTINGS pane floor + declared panes, fixing the shape the web UI renders. Still loose where no consumer pulls it (the tester's probe side, `other`'s internal shape); it tightens further as the web_server device (child b) and real devices declare panes; (b) BaseShim's one-loop primitive is now RESOLVED AND BUILT (2026-07-18, PROVEN — test_shim.py green): the shim's loop IS the per-pulse CALLBACK-firing driven by the ground_loop heartbeat. On each pulse, `on_pulse(now, context)` evaluates the device's callbacks (a batch that a bad callback cannot abort — CP2/Law 7) and pokes the target of each that fires, onto the bus; `deliver(envelope)` receives the device's mail and starts the device on demand (`_start_device`). This is what makes 'hang callbacks on the heartbeat' universal, and it is what the deferred one-loop (MAP.md:327) was waiting to become — no UU-style loop thread, because the heartbeat is the one loop (the ground_loop only beats; the shim handles the pulse). The CALLBACK is the immutable declaration (callback.py, class-space); the TICKET (mutable workflow node, instance-space) is a DIFFERENT species — LEARNING is a state, a callback is the worker a LEARNING node sets — and the ticket state-machine still waits on the emit-chokepoint (`CairnCommons/tickets/state-machine-physics.json`). The lifecycle contract (start/stop/etc.) beyond `_start_device`, and each device its own OS PROCESS (vs today's in-process instantiation), remain filed edges.
content.gates: proof gate — the two pin tests in proofs/, runnable bare (python3) today; the tester device will own how proofs run when it lands. Durability gate (code-seam): not done until proven AND committed.
content.falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) the code and the canonical commons record (`CairnCommons/intentions-other/core-values.md`) disagree on the set, order, or any narrative (per that record's own falsifier); (2) `CORE_VALUES` is not exactly [CP1..CP6] in order, or the frozen `CoreValue` becomes mutable; (3) `BaseDevice` or `BaseShim` stops composing `CoreValuesMixin`, or any imported subclass of them overrides `CORE_VALUES` to a divergent set; (4) `introspect()` stops reporting intention -> state -> settings in that order; (5) `BaseDevice` stops composing `DiagnosticBase` or `emit` stops being inherited (a device that cannot send diagnostics home breaks 'transition-grade surface everywhere'), or a homeless `emit` silently DROPS a record instead of holding it loud (Law 7 — the silent-red defect this exists to end).
content.how_it_learns: The values themselves do not learn autonomously — by design. They are a frozen, ratified contract (Law 2), the terminal floor the rest of the system stands on; nothing-is-terminal does not apply to the axioms themselves. Their horizon is human: the words change only when Akien re-ratifies them (see core-values.md horizon). The Form v0 surface *does* have a learning path, but not here: each device charter answers, for itself, which CP became which enforced check and whether that check earns its keep, and the report *shape* firms up from what its consumers (tester, web UI) actually need. The base only guarantees the six are present to be consumed and the introspection surface is ordered and uniform.

content.schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
content.what: The first real device on the spine — the system's notary and its proving mechanism (MAP.md:569). `TesterDevice` subclasses `BaseDevice` (so it carries CP1-CP6 and reports the Form v0 #2 surface). Its capabilities today: (1) `run_proof(path)` — run a proof file as a subprocess and PRODUCE a VALIDATION record (the ratified eight fields: claim, caller, date, method, verdict, evidence, falsifier, horizon); the verdict is read from the subject's exit code, not granted. (2) It OWNS THE NETWORK (MAP.md:333): `run_proof(..., isolation='netns')` runs the subject inside a MEASURED network seal (`cairn/tester/isolation.py`) — the seal is probed from inside (never assumed) and its verdict is folded into the record's `method`+`evidence`, no ninth field. An unconfirmable seal is INDETERMINATE (CP1) and never launders into a green.
content.why: Law 8 — nothing enters proven-space without a proof a hollow build couldn't pass; the verdict must come from a hand the builder did not guide. Placing that gate in a device (not a convention each builder self-applies) is Law 4 (physics over policy): the proving and the attesting come from the same hand, so a green cannot be self-granted. This is the smallest honest version of the tester — it proves, it attests, it dogfoods (its first act validates the real base proofs) — while the pillars it will grow (network ownership, a durable store, richer verdict methods) stay filed edges, built when a real need pulls them (round-three discipline: simplest provable stone first).

content.node_class: device — the first runtime component on the spine (base/ is the code-seam it stands on).
content.entry: FRESH design, MECHANISM grafted. The tester's design — device-hood, the VALIDATION record, the seal's four verdicts mapped to CP1/Law 3, the seal recorded INSIDE the VALIDATION rather than a parallel record — is Cairn's, authored to Form v0 (like device.py/shim.py). The seal's OS PLUMBING crosses nearly literally from UU's isolation.py (the `bwrap --unshare-net --cap-add CAP_NET_ADMIN` flag string, the inside-probe, the `available()` apparmor check) because it is kernel truth, not a design Cairn replaced. What did NOT cross: UU's netpolicy Router (fixture/refuse/forward + the nftables reject+counter) — the programmable-network pillar, deferred (see open edge (b)); and UU's verdict shape (seal_confirmed/seal_detail/duration_s), replaced by the Cairn VALIDATION.
content.state: PROVED for the minimal stone: (proving) TesterDevice runs a proof as a subprocess and produces a well-formed VALIDATION; a KNOWN-GREEN fixture validates green and a KNOWN-RED fixture validates red (the hollow-TESTER killer — verified by falsification: an always-green tester trips test_red_proof_validates_red; a nine-field record trips the schema tooth). (network) It runs a subject inside a MEASURED network seal; on this host the seal is LIVE-CONFIRMED (1.1.1.1:53 reachable bare, unreachable inside netns), and the hollow-SEAL killer is verified by falsification (a netns whose wrap() removes no route is caught BREACHED, not trusted; an always-SEALED measurement fails the four-verdict proof). It is the FIRST real subject the base's armed composition trap bites on. Proven green, no install. OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) CLOSED 2026-07-22 — VALIDATIONS are now PERSISTED, to beside-code git-JSON (cairn/tester/validation_store.py: validations/<stem>.json beside the proofs/ each seals, Law 5), NOT to db_domain — the ruling that moved them out of Postgres (charter-state-history-split child b). run_proof still returns the record and writes nothing itself; the standing-lesson gate persists it, so class-space stays state-free by run_proof. The append-only store refuses a drifted (non-eight-field) record; (b) the seal delivers NO ROUTE (the closed half); the CHOSEN ROUTE — a Router that serves a FIXTURE, REFUSES with a kernel counter, or FORWARDS over a Unix socket for a named dependency — is deferred, pulled by db_domain (FORWARD to Postgres, preserving peer auth) and inference_domain (FIXTURE/REFUSE). This advances but does NOT retire CLAUDE.md's 'reached only through the domain' IOUs: they retire when the domain is the SOLE route AND a kernel rule / import-scan enforces it; (c) the verdict METHOD is exit-code + seal only — quorum / review-by-N-experts for concept pieces (MAP.md:752) is a later method the `method` field names room for; (d) reported-vs-filed intention drift detection is not wired.

content.why: The founding law (MAP.md:367, generalizing UU's button pattern; Law 6): every table has exactly one owner and the owner gates every write; an ownerless table cannot come into existence. This is PHYSICS, not policy (Law 4): the owner registry carries `CHECK (owner <> '')`, so Postgres itself rejects an ownerless row, and `create_owned_table` is the only door — a table that skipped the registry did not come from db_domain, and db_domain is the only module holding a connection. content.what: THE HEARTBEAT — the most basic mechanism in Cairn: ONE daemon that provides a pulse, and nothing more. It does not execute, resolve, schedule, route, or write. On each `beat(now, context)` it pulses the SHIM of every subscribed device, in subscription order. Handling a pulse — evaluating the device's CALLBACKS (each trigger where its data is owned, Law 6), firing the due ones by poking the bus — lives in the shim (`cairn/base/shim.py`), NOT here and not in the device body. The ground loop only beats. It also PUBLISHES ITS ROSTER (`roster()`, web-server child c) at all times — the devices it beats to, in order, each with live wakefulness — as the authoritative nav for the web presentation surface; no new owner and no new state, the roster IS the subscription list this device already owns (Law 6).
content.why: A single daemon structure that everyone else hangs their own handlers on — so no device runs a bespoke loop or daemon of its own (the UU disease: dozens of independent daemons rotting silently). One heartbeat; every device's shim hangs its callbacks on it. The decisive property, and the reason the ground loop stays this small: the CALLBACK IS THE SAME UNIT whether it fires on a scheduled cadence or on a device's own internal trigger — same shape, same firing path in the shim, only the owner and location of its data differ. Keeping the ground loop to just-the-beat is what makes that sameness hold. The heartbeat holds NO runtime state of its own; all durable state lives in db_domain, reached by the devices the shim wakes.
content.invoke: Construct: `gl = GroundLoopDevice()`. A device subscribes its shim: `gl.subscribe(shim)` (idempotent by device_id; only a shim — device_id + on_pulse — may subscribe). Beat: `gl.beat(now, context)` pulses every subscribed shim once and returns a legible beat-record. `now` is passed explicitly, so the pulse is provable without a clock. Prove: `python3 cairn/ground_loop/proofs/test_ground_loop.py` (exit 0 = green).
content.node_class: device — but the MINIMAL one: the heartbeat daemon the whole loop-system rests on. It is the first of the two universal substrates (the heartbeat; the bus is the second). It is NOT an executor; the executor/firing role belongs to the shim (that conflation was the goof this charter corrects).
content.entry: FRESH design to Form v0. The mechanism is Cairn's own: one daemon, one beat, firing-in-the-shim. No mechanism grafted.
content.state: IMPLEMENTED to the heartbeat shape and PROVEN (green bare and under the tester). The first cut (cairn `584aa74`) built the ground loop as a generic DRIVER-EXECUTOR (`run_driver`: resolve a proven method → run it → write a row to an owned target, backed by a method-registry). That was the goof: the executor role, the firing, and the heartbeat got collapsed into one device, losing the property that a callback is the same unit no matter what fires it (Akien, 2026-07-18). The correction is now BUILT: the ground loop is ONLY the heartbeat (`beat` pulses subscribed shims and leaves a beat-record); the resolve-and-fire logic moved into the shim's per-pulse callback-firing (`cairn/base/shim.py`); the method-registry + collect fixtures + the executor proof were RETIRED with the goof; the central-scheduler framing (system_rackmount) dissolves into this one universal mechanism. Proven: a beat pulses every subscribed shim in order; the firing is the shim's (a due callback pokes the bus THROUGH its shim, the heartbeat pokes nothing); subscribe is idempotent + typed; one shim raising cannot stop the beat reaching the others (CP2, Law 7); the executor is gone (no run_driver, no registry); it is a device (Form v0 #2). ADDED 2026-07-22 (web-server child c, PROVEN — test_ground_loop.py green under the tester): `roster()` publishes the live nav at all times — the subscribed devices in order, each with live wakefulness (its shim's `running`), available before the first beat too; a device absent from subscriptions is absent from the roster (you navigate to what the heartbeat beats); the roster is DATA the web server renders. Also `shim_for(id)` — a read accessor returning a subscribed shim (or None) so the web presentation surface reaches a device's page THROUGH the heartbeat that holds it (in-process v0; over-the-bus is the filed separate-process edge). No new owner — both are the subscription list this device already owns. OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) SUBSCRIPTION by a file in the device's own folder tree — today `subscribe(shim)` in-process; discovering subscribers by scanning device code trees for a callback-declaration file grows when devices are separate OS processes; (b) the wall-clock DAEMON that pulses `beat` on a real cadence — the OS-specific backing, a thin wrapper this stays provable without (like sudo_relay's daemon.py).
content.gates: proof gate (tester) — proofs/test_ground_loop.py, runnable bare (python3) and under the tester. Durability gate: not done until proven AND committed.
content.falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) the ground loop does MORE than beat — if it resolves a method, runs a callback, writes state, or routes a poke itself, it has re-absorbed the shim's role (the goof); (2) firing lives anywhere but the shim — a device that must implement its own loop to get its callbacks fired breaks 'built into all devices'; (3) `subscribe` accepts a non-shim (no on_pulse), or double-subscribes the same device_id; (4) a callback fired on a cadence differs in shape or handling from one fired on a device's own internal trigger (the sameness is the whole point); (5) the heartbeat holds runtime state of its own instead of only beating (durable state is db_domain's, reached by the woken devices).
content.how_it_learns: The heartbeat itself does not learn — it is the floor everything beats on (like the core values, a deliberate terminal simplicity). Learning happens in the CALLBACKS the shims hang on it: each callback is a handler whose firing leaves evidence on the bus. The lesson this component itself carries is a specimen of learning-its-gates: collapsing the heartbeat, the executor, and the scheduler into one device was caught by Akien at n=1 and corrected before it hardened — the ground loop learned to be smaller (its `run_driver` and registry were deleted, not patched). Its horizon: whether one heartbeat daemon + shim-fired callbacks covers a scheduled cadence AND a device's own internal triggers with the SAME callback — measured as more real callbacks land (the system device's threshold pokes are the first).

content.what: THE SYSTEM DEVICE — the rack-mount device that owns the host's SYSTEM predicates and serves them device-independently. It owns host-resource data (CPU, memory, disk) and ADVERTISES the callbacks it can serve against them ('I accept a cpu_threshold callback, takes a value'). A caller SUBSCRIBES ('I'll take one, value 80, here's my callback address'); the system device RESOLVES ITS OWN METHOD internally (the caller never learns object.object.method), evaluates the predicate LOCALLY (Law 6 — the trigger reads the host metric INSIDE the device; the caller never sees the raw number), and its SHIM pokes the subscriber on the heartbeat pulse when the line is crossed. `SystemRackmountDevice` subclasses `BaseDevice` (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2, on which the callback menu is advertised); `SystemRackmountShim` subclasses `BaseShim` and fires the subscriptions on the beat. It is NOT a central scheduler — scheduling is the universal heartbeat + shim + callback mechanism.
content.why: So no device is coupled to the host, and no host metric is exported just to be tested (Law 6): any device can get a trigger on a host resource, but the reading STAYS HOME — only the wake-up (the poke) crosses the bus. The OS-specific backing of a host reading (load average vs a richer sampler; and, in the same family, privilege via sudo_relay) is swapped BEHIND the advertised callbacks without changing any device that depends on them. This is 'abstract host services device-independently' made concrete — and it corrects the goof: the system rackmount is NOT the rack (the rack is the chassis: bus + shims), and it is NOT a central scheduler (the interval/date/quantity/state enum + the SchedulerService were deleted). It is simply the OWNER of one kind of data other devices want triggers on.
content.invoke: Construct: `dev = SystemRackmountDevice(sampler=None)` (inject a sampler in proofs). Inspect offerings: `dev.advertises()`. Subscribe: `dev.subscribe('cpu_threshold', address='ops/personal', why=..., value=80)` → a subscription id. Wire it to the beat: `shim = SystemRackmountShim(dev, bus); ground_loop.subscribe(shim)` — the shim pokes subscribers whose line is crossed on each `beat`. Prove: `python3 cairn/system_rackmount/proofs/test_system_rackmount.py` (exit 0 = green).
content.node_class: device — a rack-mount device on the spine (the spine step after ground_loop). It is the owner of the host's resource predicates; it serves threshold callbacks on them through the universal heartbeat + shim + bus mechanism.
content.entry: FRESH design to Form v0. The METAPHOR is quarried from UU (a rack holds rack-mount devices; a bus connects them; system services are abstracted so devices are portable), but the shape is Cairn's: a BaseDevice that OWNS host-resource data and ADVERTISES callbacks on it, resolving its own method so a caller stays ignorant of internals, and poking through the shared heartbeat + bus rather than a bespoke scheduler. What is NEW and Cairn's: advertise → subscribe → poke as the registration protocol; Law-6-local-evaluation (the host metric never leaves the device); the system device as a data-OWNER, not a service-registry or scheduler.
content.state: IMPLEMENTED to the system-device shape and PROVEN (green bare and under the tester). It advertises the cpu_threshold callback; a caller subscribes by menu name + value + address and an unadvertised name is refused loudly (CP1); the device resolves its own predicate internally and evaluates it LOCALLY; end to end through the heartbeat, a beat with the host over the line pokes the subscriber's feed on the real bus exactly once and a beat under the line pokes no one; the poke body carries the caller's own line (80) but the raw reading (95) crosses the bus NOWHERE (Law 6, asserted); the central-scheduler goof is gone (no service()/scheduler API, no trigger-kind enum); it is a device and its shim is a shim (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2). OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) only cpu_threshold is advertised (via a dependency-free load-average sampler); memory/disk predicates are the SAME shape and mount when a consumer asks; (b) subscriptions are LEVEL-triggered (poke while the predicate holds) — edge-triggering and unsubscribe wait on a real need; (c) advertise/subscribe are direct calls today — expressing them as bus messages (like the poke already is) is a filed edge; (d) the real host sampler (rich per-core CPU, memory, disk) is the OS-specific backing this device will own — a thin edge; the predicate physics is proven by injection; (e) sudo_relay (privilege) is a host service in this same family, built standalone first — it migrates behind this abstraction when a second consumer makes the seam pay.

content.falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) the device serves a callback it did not advertise, or accepts a subscription to an unadvertised name instead of refusing loudly (CP1); (2) a poke fires when the host is UNDER the caller's line, or fails to fire when it is over (the predicate physics must be exact); (3) THE RAW READING CROSSES THE BUS — a poke body (or any envelope) carries the host metric rather than only that the line was crossed (Law 6 broken — the whole point); (4) the device pokes subscribers ITSELF rather than through its shim on the heartbeat pulse (re-absorbing the firing the shim owns), or re-grows a central scheduler / a trigger-kind enum; (5) `SystemRackmountDevice`/`SystemRackmountShim` stop subclassing `BaseDevice`/`BaseShim` / drop CP1-CP6, or `introspect()` stops reporting intention → state → settings in order.
content.how_it_learns: Yes. (1) The subscriptions + the pokes ARE the learning substrate: the device's state() surfaces who is waiting on which line (never the reading), and every poke is a why-bearing record on the bus — a threshold that never fires and one that fires constantly are both signals in the record, not in silence. (2) Law-6-local-evaluation is itself a learning stance: by keeping the host's data home and exporting only the wake-up, the system learns which host predicates it actually needs to advertise (cpu first, memory/disk as consumers ask) without ever coupling a consumer to the metric — the same start-narrow-and-let-evidence-widen discipline as sudo_relay's box. Its own horizon: the advertised menu (cpu today) and the level-triggered semantics are best-guesses-with-an-expiry — they firm up as real consumers (an ops device, the harvester, a memory-pressure watcher) subscribe.

intentions.content:
# The heartbeat, the callback, and the bus

*Ratified in conversation with Akien, 2026-07-18 (evening). This is the converged
runtime architecture for how Cairn devices are driven and how they talk. It sharpens
and partly supersedes the earlier "operational-driver primitive" + "host/rack" +
"peers fire in two modes" sketches in `MAP.md` and `tickets/state-machine-physics.json`.
The code shipped 2026-07-18 morning (`ground_loop` `584aa74`, `system_rackmount` `5cb593a`)
was built to the OLD shape (a generic `run_driver` executor + a central scheduler) — that
shape was the goof this document corrects. The rework SHIPPED the same evening (commits
`d27ce1d` bus, `ae2d372` base callback+shim, `a535de0` heartbeat, `faafb70` system device),
each piece proven under the tester — see "What this changes in code — DONE" at the bottom.*

## The two universal substrates

Everything runtime hangs on exactly two things:

- **The heartbeat** — one daemon that provides a pulse, and nothing more. This is the
  `ground_loop`. It does not execute, resolve, schedule, or route. It beats.
- **The bus** — one common messaging substrate for everything. Inspectable, logged,
  and the *sole* path for inter-device communication.

The symmetry that makes them load-bearing:

| Substrate | Sole path to… | Owner-gated | Inspectable/logged by construction |
|---|---|---|---|
| `db_domain` | durable **state** | yes (Law 6) | yes |
| **the bus** | inter-device **communication** | yes (Law 6) | yes |
| the heartbeat | — (it only beats) | — | the beat is trivially observable |

Because the bus is the *only* door for communication (devices never hold references
to each other, never call each other directly), "inspectable + logged + common" are
not features added per surface — they are automatic. Physics, not policy (Law 4).

## Devices, shims, and processes

- **Each device is its own process.** Isolated, independently crash/restartable, and
  it does **not** run continuously — it sleeps when idle and is woken on demand.
- **The shim** (`BaseShim`) is always-on, lightweight, one per device. It:
  1. fires the device's due callbacks on each heartbeat pulse,
  2. **receives** incoming bus messages for its device,
  3. **starts the device** (the heavier process) on demand when a message arrives and
     the device isn't running.
  So the shim is the device's persistent front and process-manager; the device is the
  heavier process the shim wakes. This is the sleep/wake peer model made physical: a
  device is a process that *wakes to a poke*, not a daemon that spins.

### Why event, not poll — the shrinking-footprint aim (Akien, 2026-07-22)

**Polling costs CPU; events don't.** A process that wakes on a schedule to check
whether something happened burns cycles on every tick where the answer is *no* — and
the answer is *no* almost every tick, by construction. An event-driven wake burns cycles
only when the thing actually happened. So the choice between "scan periodically" and
"subscribe to the gate that produces the change" is not a style preference — it is the
difference between a cost that grows with the *clock* and a cost that grows with *real
events*, and real events are rare relative to the clock.

This traces to a standing aim Akien named explicitly: **an ever-decreasing computational
footprint.** Cairn should cost *less* to run as it matures, not more — the opposite of a
system that accretes background daemons and cron ticks until the machine is busy doing
nothing. Every "poll" we can convert to an "event" moves a cost off the clock and onto
the events that justify it.

**Where it traces (Akien, 2026-07-22): telos aims 1 and 5.** It is a *derived
consequence*, not a seventh aim and not a core value. Aim **1 (demonstrate inference
compilation)** is the load-bearing parent, because **Law 1 is this same principle stated
at the inference level** — "the resolver is spent on the novel, not on re-deriving the
settled" is *don't spend compute twice*. Inference compilation turns re-derivation into
structure, which *is* reduced compute; event-not-poll is that principle one layer down, at
runtime instead of at inference. Improving along that axis as the system matures is aim
**5 (self-improving).** So shrinking-footprint is compilation generalized from inference
to the running machine — not, primarily, an altruism (aim 6) thing.

It is also **a why for the ground loop itself**, though the ground loop's charter does
not phrase it this way: the ground loop is the *one* permitted periodic pulse, and it
exists precisely so that nothing else has to be. One heartbeat, shared, replaces every
component's private timer — N polling loops collapse to one, and the one does nothing but
beat. Concretely, this is why the pre-Cairn cron jobs were deleted (2026-07-22) rather
than ported: a cron entry is a private polling loop with a private schedule and no owner
(see `notes/held-inspectors-janitors-filters.json` — the unowned-default that governs a
record's survival). Functionality like `cleanupPeriodDays` may well be rebuilt — but it
will hang on a callback fired by a gate, not on a clock that wakes to ask "anything?".

## Callbacks vs tickets — two species, named for what they are

- **Callback** — *immutable, no workflow.* "Call X when this trigger is true." It
  carries no state of its own. Because it is *declaration*, it lives with the device's
  **code** (class-space — git, greppable, shareable). A callback firing = **kick off a
  separate, short-lived Python process** in its own space that sends a message to the
  target's shim, then **terminates.** That statelessness is why it can be a fire-and-die
  process. The heartbeat fires callbacks (via the shim). Every recurring wake-up —
  interval, time, data-accumulated, resource-threshold — is a callback.
- **Ticket** — *a workflow node: mutable, carries a state machine* (a journey, e.g.
  `LEARNING → ARCHIVED`). A different species. Its state is mutable, so it lives where
  workflow-state lives (instance-space / the node store), not class-space.
- **`LEARNING` is a state; a callback is the worker.** A node *in* `LEARNING` can *set*
  a callback. The state is the condition the node rests in; the callback is the doing.
  When the work ends (e.g. an expiry trigger fires), the state moves (`→ ARCHIVED`) and
  the callback is unset. "This node is `LEARNING`" (a state fact) and "this node has set
  a callback" (a worker it owns) are two separate things — do not mush them into "a
  mutable ticket."

Callbacks are general, not scheduler-specific: even the question-nexus template's loop
is a callback. One primitive for "call this again / on this trigger," used everywhere.

## Triggers — anything that evaluates to true

**A trigger is anything that can evaluate to true.** A predicate. There is **no closed
enum** of trigger kinds (the shipped `interval/date/quantity/state` set was a
reification — see the reify-vs-flow catches below). Open examples, not a taxonomy:

- an interval elapsed, or a specific wall-clock time arrived (the "cron" subset)
- an amount of data accumulated past a threshold (queue depth — this is also how
  **backpressure** works: a filling channel fires a callback on its reader)
- a resource crossing a line — CPU pegged, memory, disk
- a state or event entered
- **a proof going green** — a proof is *precisely* a claim evaluated to true (a green
  VALIDATION), so proof is the exemplar quality-trigger; `PROVEME → PROVED` is a
  proof-trigger. The tester (Law 8) and the callback system are the same substrate.

New signal → new predicate, **not a schema change.**

## Where a callback is evaluated — Law 6 for triggers

**A callback is evaluated wherever its trigger's data is owned.** So device-local data
is never exported; only the *wake-up* crosses the bus, never the raw data it tested.

- **Global** callbacks read genuinely shared data (the passage of time / the beat).
  Evaluated at the heartbeat level.
- **Device-specific** callbacks read a device's own data (its queue depth, an internal
  metric). Evaluated *inside* the device; the data stays home.

Consequences: minimal data movement (telemetry never crosses the bus just to be
tested), and encapsulation by construction.

## Advertise → subscribe → poke (the registration protocol)

A caller never needs to know a device's internals (`object.object.method`):

1. A device **advertises a menu** of callbacks it offers — e.g. "I accept a CPU-threshold
   callback (takes a value)" — as one item in a list of its offerings (part of its
   Form v0 #2 surface: inspect a device, see what callbacks it offers).
2. A caller **subscribes**: *"I'll take one of those, value 80, here's my callback
   address."*
3. The device **resolves its own method internally** and wires it into the callback.
4. When the predicate goes true, the device **pokes the caller's address**. The caller
   gets its wake-up and never saw a CPU number.

Owner-gated throughout (Law 6). Advertise / subscribe / poke are all just bus messages.

Worked example: *"alert me at 80% CPU."* CPU is the **system device's** data (it is the
one that can make the CPU calls). So the CPU-threshold callback lives on the system
device; any device that wants it asks the system device (owner-gated), which evaluates
locally and pokes the requester when true. This is what "abstracts host services
device-independently" cashes out to — **`system_rackmount` = the system device: owner
of host-resource predicates (CPU/memory/disk), serving threshold callbacks on request.**
NOT a central scheduler. (Settles the earlier open A/B question → A.)

## The bus in detail

- **Protocol at the edge.** The bus's semantics (channels, owned envelopes, retention,
  causality) are Cairn's; the wire protocol is a *swappable adapter*. MCP is the current
  lingua franca for agentic communication, so it is the adapter today — swap it when the
  ecosystem moves, the way `system_rackmount` abstracts an OS service behind a
  device-independent face. The protocol must not hold the design hostage.
- **One substrate; every surface is a view.** UU split logic between the mcp bus and
  "how the web server surfaced things." Cairn: the web server, the MCP inspector, a
  debug pane are all *read-projections* of the one bus. Nothing surfaced by re-deriving
  it elsewhere (Law 1).
- **Durable transit rides `db_domain`, owner-gated.** The bus does not open its own
  Postgres; a message in transit is an owned write through `db_domain`. That buys
  logged + inspectable for free and enforces one-owner.
- **Record-of-truth vs diagnostic surface, as physics (Law 7).** Permanent records
  (errors, announces-of-fact) never collapse and never expire; diagnostic channels
  (debug/info) may collapse in a view and expire on a rolling window (sudo_relay's
  pattern). The substrate always stores the full truth; only views collapse.
- **Channels per device** (Murderbot-feeds model, Martha Wells): **announce** (public
  feed — public conversations), **personal** (chat inbox — others post through the
  owner's gate), **info** + **debug** (logging). A device's `introspect()` surface can
  publish onto its announce feed, so *inspecting a device is reading its feed* —
  observability and messaging stop being two systems.
- **Every envelope carries why + causality** (sender, intention, reply-to), so the bus
  is a *replayable causal record*, not just traffic. A device woken from sleep rebuilds
  its context by reading its own feed history (horizon-of-awareness, made concrete).
- **The human is a native participant.** Akien has channels like any device; the web
  server is a view. Participation on the bus, not operation through a side door
  (get-Akien-out-of-ops at the messaging layer).

## The reify-vs-flow catches this session (specimens of learning-its-gates)

CC (I) froze Cairn-fluid categories three times; Akien caught each at n=1:

1. **rack = scheduler** — inferred from one MAP line; the rack is the *chassis* (bus +
   shims), not a scheduler.
2. **ground_loop = executor** — collapsed the heartbeat, the ticket-firing, and the
   scheduler into one device, losing "the ticket is the same unit no matter what fires
   it." The ground loop is *only* the heartbeat; firing lives in the shim.
3. **triggers = `interval/date/quantity/state` enum** — froze an open list of example
   predicates into a closed typed set and coined "quantity." A trigger is *anything that
   evaluates to true.*

The tell: I turn examples/metaphors into frozen taxonomies. The fix flows them apart.

## What this changes in code — DONE (reworked + proven 2026-07-18 evening)

The rework shipped the night the model converged, each piece proven bare AND under the
tester, each committed separately:

- the **bus** → BUILT (`cairn/bus/`, commit `d27ce1d`). The sole comms path; durable transit
  rides db_domain (owner `bus`); per-device channels (announce/personal records, info/debug
  diagnostic); record channels refuse to collapse, diagnostic views may (Law 7); every
  envelope carries why + causality (Law 5). Filed: MCP wire-adapter; per-device-owned channels.
- the **Callback primitive** → BUILT (`cairn/base/callback.py`, commit `ae2d372`). Immutable;
  a trigger is ANY predicate `(now, context) -> bool`, NOT a named kind (the enum is deleted);
  evaluated where its data is owned (Law 6).
- `BaseShim` → REWORKED (`cairn/base/shim.py`, `ae2d372`). Gains per-pulse callback-firing
  (`on_pulse`, batch-safe), message receipt + on-demand device start (`deliver`/`_start_device`).
  The long-deferred one-loop primitive is resolved: the heartbeat IS the one loop. Filed: each
  device its own OS process (the shape — start-on-demand — is proven; real spawn grows against need).
- `ground_loop` → STRIPPED to the heartbeat (`cairn/ground_loop/loop.py`, commit `a535de0`).
  `beat(now, context)` pulses subscribed shims; no `run_driver`, no resolve, no write. The
  method-registry + collect fixtures + executor proof were RETIRED (the proven-space registry
  returns with the emit-chokepoint when a real consumer pulls it).
- `system_rackmount` → REWORKED to the *system device* (`cairn/system_rackmount/`, commit
  `faafb70`). Owns host-resource predicates; advertise → subscribe → poke; evaluates locally so
  the reading never leaves (Law 6); the central `SchedulerService` + `interval/date/quantity/
  state` enum are DELETED. Its capstone proof composes every piece above end-to-end.
- **tickets** → the mutable workflow species stays DISTINCT and DEFERRED — the callback/ticket
  boundary is now clean in code (a callback is the immutable worker; a ticket is the mutable
  node), but the ticket state machine still waits on the emit-chokepoint
  (`CairnCommons/tickets/state-machine-physics.json`). Not built here, by design (not the goof).

content.schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
content.what: THE SECOND SUBSTRATE — the one common messaging substrate for everything, and the SOLE path for inter-device communication. Devices never hold references to each other or call each other directly; they `post` (the only way to send) and `read` (the record — inspecting a device is reading its feed). Each device has four channels: `announce` + `personal` (RECORDS of truth) and `info` + `debug` (DIAGNOSTIC). Every envelope carries its why (CP3) and its causality (`sender`, `reply_to`), so the bus is a replayable causal record. Durable transit rides `db_domain` (owner `bus`, Law 6) — the bus opens no connection of its own. `BusDevice` subclasses `BaseDevice` (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2).
content.why: Because communication has EXACTLY ONE DOOR, 'inspectable + logged + common' are not features bolted onto each surface — they are automatic, by construction (Law 4). This is the exact symmetry of db_domain: db_domain is the sole path to durable STATE (owner-gated, logged); the bus is the sole path to inter-device COMMUNICATION (owner-gated, logged). Every later surface — a web feed, an MCP inspector, a debug pane — is a READ-PROJECTION of this one substrate, so observability is never a separate build and nothing is re-derived elsewhere (Law 1). A woken device rebuilds its context by reading its own feed history (horizon-of-awareness, made concrete).
content.invoke: Construct: `bus = BusDevice()` (or `BusDevice(table=...)` for an ephemeral/proof table). Send: `bus.post(sender=..., to=..., channel='personal', why=..., body={...}, reply_to=None)` — the sole path. Inspect: `bus.read(to='dev', channel='announce')` returns the full-truth feed; `bus.digest(to='dev', channel='debug')` collapses a DIAGNOSTIC channel to count + tail (it refuses to collapse a record channel). Prove: `python3 cairn/bus/proofs/test_bus.py` (exit 0 = green).
content.node_class: device — the second universal substrate on the spine, the peer of db_domain. Where db_domain owns durable state, the bus owns inter-device communication.
content.entry: FRESH design, metaphor quarried from UU (the Murderbot-feeds model — announce/personal/info/debug per device — and UU's Postgres-backed message bus with envelopes). What is NEW and Cairn's: comms as a SUBSTRATE with a single door (post), symmetric with db_domain; durable transit RIDING db_domain rather than opening its own store (one-owner + logged for free); the record-vs-diagnostic distinction enforced as physics (Law 7) — a record channel refuses to collapse, only a diagnostic VIEW may; every envelope carrying why + causality so the bus is a replayable causal record, not raw traffic.
content.state: IMPLEMENTED to v0 and PROVEN (green bare and under the tester). A message posts as an owned transit row through db_domain and reads back whole, with why + reply_to intact and in causal order; an unknown channel is refused (CP1); a whyless message is refused (CP3); a record channel refuses to collapse into a digest while a diagnostic channel collapses to count + tail — yet `read` still returns the full truth (the substrate never collapses, only the view does, Law 7); the transit table is owned by `bus` and a non-bus write is refused by db_domain's gate (Law 6); it is a device (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2). OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) the WIRE PROTOCOL is a swappable adapter — the semantics are Cairn's; an MCP adapter at the edge is a filed edge (the substrate must not be held hostage to a protocol); (b) PER-DEVICE-OWNED channels — today the bus owns one transit table and is its sole writer, attributing each sender; making each device the owner of its own inbound channel waits on a real multi-owner need; (c) RETENTION — the digest view collapses now, a durable rolling-window expiry of diagnostic channels waits on real volume; (d) the HUMAN as a native participant (Akien gets channels like any device) — the channel shape is here, wiring Akien's feeds is the web-server stone.
content.gates: proof gate — proofs/test_bus.py, runnable bare (python3) and under the tester. Durability gate: not done until proven AND committed.
content.falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) a posted message does not read back, or loses its why / sender / reply_to (a hollow bus that dropped or forgot a message — Law 5); (2) an unknown channel is accepted, or a whyless message is accepted (CP1 / CP3); (3) a RECORD channel collapses into a digest, OR a diagnostic `digest` alters what `read` returns (the substrate collapsing rather than only the view — Law 7); (4) a non-`bus` writer can write the transit table (the owner-gate not holding — Law 6), or the bus opens its own DB connection instead of riding db_domain; (5) `BusDevice` stops subclassing `BaseDevice` / drops CP1-CP6, or `introspect()` stops reporting intention → state → settings in order.
content.how_it_learns: Yes. (1) The bus IS the learning substrate for the whole fleet: because every inter-device message passes through one door as an owned, why-bearing, causal record, the trail of what the system actually said and why is greppable evidence, not memory — a device that stops posting is a signal, a channel that floods is a signal, both visible in the record rather than in silence (the UU split-between-bus-and-web-server disease, closed). (2) The record-vs-diagnostic seam is itself a learning stance: by naming which channels are records of truth (never collapse) and which are diagnostic (may collapse) BEFORE there are many surfaces, the system learns which surfaces it needs and adds them as VIEWS of the one substrate, never re-deriving traffic elsewhere. Its own horizon: the four channels and the digest shape are best-guesses-with-an-expiry — they firm up as real consumers (the system device's pokes, the web server's feeds, an MCP adapter) exercise them.

Law 2 (a device carries CP1-CP6 structurally)
Law 4 (single-door comms makes inspectable+logged+common physics, not per-surface policy)
Law 6 (durable transit is an owner-gated write through db_domain)
Law 7 (record-of-truth channels never collapse; only a diagnostic VIEW may — the substrate always stores full truth)
Law 5 (intent + code + proofs share this address; and every envelope carries its why)
Law 1 (every surface is a read-projection of the one substrate — nothing re-derived elsewhere)
the converged architecture (CairnCommons/intentions-other/I-heartbeat-callbacks-and-bus.md — the two substrates)
db_domain (the peer substrate the bus is symmetric with, and rides for transit)
content.owner: CC owns the bus code + its proofs and gates writes to them. The bus owns its transit table (owner `bus`) through db_domain; it is the sole writer of traffic, on behalf of attributed senders.
